text: The surgeon places three 5mm ports and one 12mm port through the patient's previous surgical scars, preparing for laparoscopic access.
The surgeon inspects the abdomen, finding no adhesions, and reflects the greater omentum cephalad to reveal the jejuno-jejunostomy site.
The video shows the roux limb prolapsing into the common channel at the site of intermittent intussusception, visually confirming the location of the issue.

The surgeon inspects and traces the small bowel limbs to their origins, visually identifying the roux limb traveling through the mesocolon, the biliopancreatic limb emerging from the ligament of Treitz, and the common channel extending distally to the cecum.
The surgeon closes the transverse colon defect, Peterson space, and small bowel mesentery, ensuring these areas do not present any visible sources of obstruction.
The surgeon begins the revision by resecting the jejuno-jejunostomy, starting with the biliopancreatic limb. A 60mm Endo GIA stapler with a 2.5mm staple cartridge is used to transect the tissue.
The surgeon marks the biliopancreatic limb with a single long suture, making it easily identifiable within the surgical field.
The surgeon transects the roux limb proximal to the anastomosis using a surgical stapler, marking the transected limb with a double suture for identification.
The surgeon staples off the common limb, completing the transection of the small bowel limbs.
The surgeon transects the mesentery using vascular two millimeter reloads, visibly separating the mesenteric tissue.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into an endocatch bag for removal from the surgical field.
The surgeon displays the roux limb, common limb, and biliopancreatic limb, all visibly free and separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to open the small bowel mesenteric defect, previously closed during the original bypass operation, to further mobilize the biliopancreatic limb.
The surgeon reconstructs the jejuno-jejunostomy in two steps, visually aligning and connecting the jejunal segments to restore continuity.

The surgeon places a stay suture and creates a side-to-side functional end-to-end anastomosis between the roux limb and the common channel using a triple stapling technique. Two firings of a 2.5 mm endo-GIA stapler, each approximately 45 mm and positioned 180 degrees apart, are followed by closure with a final 60 mm endo-GIA stapler with a white load.
The surgeon closes the new mesenteric defect between the root and common limbs using a running 3-0 silk suture, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
The surgeon performs an anastomosis between the biliopancreatic limb and the common limb, approximately ten centimeters distal to the previously created anastomosis.
The surgeon closes the new mesenteric defect between the biliopancreatic limb and the common limb using a running 3-0 silk suture.
The surgeon verifies the anatomy, tracing the retrocolic Roux limb to the common channel and its anastomosis with the biliopancreatic limb, following the pathway back to the ligament of Treitz.
The surgeon visually inspects both anastomoses, confirming their patency, with no signs of obstruction or leakage.
